title: Two Pointers & Sliding Window
summary: Two pointers turn overlapping $O(n^2)$ subarray scans into a single $O(n)$ pass — one family walks inward from both ends of sorted data, the other slides a window that grows on the right and shrinks on the left. Flipkart GRiD and Microsoft Codility lean heavily on these mediums, so drill this module for **speed**, not just understanding — the OA-flagged problems below are the most likely to appear in your actual assessments.

notes.heading: When this pattern fires
notes.body:
Every problem in this module has the same skeleton: the answer lives in a **contiguous** chunk of an array or string, and brute force would re-scan overlapping ranges over and over. Two pointers and sliding windows are how you stop paying for that overlap.

Reach for this pattern when you spot any of these tells:

- The input is **contiguous** (array, string) and the question is about a **subarray / substring** — not a subsequence.
- Words like **longest / shortest / count of** a window satisfying a constraint: sum at most K, at most K distinct, no repeated characters.
- The array is **sorted** (or can be sorted) and you need a **pair or triple** hitting a target — that is opposite pointers.
- The brute force is an $O(n^2)$ or $O(n^3)$ scan over all subarrays or pairs, and each step's work **overlaps** the previous step's. That overlap is exactly what a moving window reuses.
- You need an **in-place** partition: move zeroes, dedupe a sorted array, Dutch national flag.

> [!ANALOGY]
> A sliding window is a caterpillar crawling along the array: the head (`right`) stretches forward to eat new elements, and when the body gets too heavy — the window turns invalid — the tail (`left`) scoots up. Neither end ever moves backwards.

> [!KEY]
> Subarray/substring = contiguous = window territory. If the problem says **subsequence**, elements need not be adjacent, the window intuition breaks, and you are usually looking at DP or greedy instead. Check this before writing a single line.

> [!EXAM]
> Flipkart GRiD and Microsoft's Codility sets love sliding-window mediums. The OA-flagged problems in this ladder are among the highest-probability questions in your whole plan — drill them until you can re-type the solution from a blank editor in minutes.

notes.heading: Family 1: opposite-direction pointers
Family 2: the sliding window (shrink-when-invalid)
Fixed-size windows: k is given to you
Longest vs shortest: where you record the answer
notes.body:
The variable-window skeleton has one switch that flips between the two big use cases, and getting it backwards returns wrong answers with no crash to warn you.

| | **Longest** valid window | **Shortest** valid window |
|---|---|---|
| Shrink when | window is **invalid** | window is **still valid** (greedily) |
| Shrink loop | `while (invalid)` | `while (valid)` |
| Record answer | **after** the shrink loop | **inside** the shrink loop |
| Example | Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters | Minimum Size Subarray Sum |

For *longest*, the window sits at its maximum valid size right after shrinking, so you measure it then. For *shortest*, every moment the window is valid is a candidate — you squeeze it as small as it will go, measuring at each squeeze (full walkthrough in the deep dives).

> [!KEY]
> Before you code, say which side you are on: *longest-valid — shrink on invalid, record after* or *shortest-valid — shrink while valid, record inside*. One sentence, and it saves twenty minutes of debugging.

> [!NOTE]
> The shrink-while-valid form needs **monotonicity**: adding an element must only push the window toward valid, removing must only push it away (e.g. positive numbers with the invariant sum ≥ target). Negative numbers break that — then you need prefix sums plus a monotonic deque instead. Naming this caveat unprompted is an interview point-scorer.

notes.heading: Pitfalls, edge cases, and how to narrate it
notes.body:
**The classic bugs**, ranked by how often they cost real points:

- **Window length is `right - left + 1`**, not `right - left`. Write it once, trust it forever.
- **Asymmetric add/remove** — every `left++` must exactly undo its matching `right` update (see Family 2 above).
- **Freq-map distinct counting:** when a count hits 0, decrement your distinct counter or `erase` the key — otherwise `map.size()` overcounts distinct elements forever.
- **3Sum-style dedupe:** after emitting a valid pair or triple, skip duplicates on *both* pointers — `while (lo < hi && a[lo] == a[lo+1]) lo++;` — or you output repeats.
- **Overflow:** `long long` for sums, always.
- **Longest/shortest confusion** — the recording-point switch from the previous section.

**Edge cases to name before the interviewer asks:** empty input, `k = 0`, `k` larger than the array, all-equal elements, a single element, unreachable target (return `0` or `-1` per the spec), and negative numbers — do they break the monotonicity your window relies on?

> [!EXAM]
> The narration script that wins these problems:
> 1. Name the pattern: the answer is a contiguous subarray and brute force re-scans overlapping ranges — sliding window.
> 2. Define the invariant precisely *before* coding: window has at most K distinct; window sum ≥ target.
> 3. State the complexity up front: each index enters and leaves once, so $O(n)$ despite the nested loop.
> 4. For opposite-pointer greedy moves, give the **exchange argument** — why the discarded side can never beat what you keep. Interviewers grade the justification, not just the code.

> [!KEY]
> If you see **exactly K** or **count the subarrays**, immediately float `exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1)` out loud. It is the highest-leverage trick in this module (see the first deep dive), and mentioning it signals real pattern fluency.

problems.name: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
problems.note: Opposite pointers on sorted data, the base case: sum too small → `lo++`, too big → `hi--`.

problems.name: Valid Palindrome
problems.note: Opposite pointers that skip non-alphanumeric characters before each comparison.

problems.name: Move Zeroes
problems.note: Slow/fast same-direction write pointer — an in-place partition keeping the non-zero prefix.

problems.name: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
problems.note: The slow write pointer owns the compacted prefix; copy forward only when the value changes.

problems.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
problems.note: One pass tracking min-so-far — a degenerate window where only the left edge matters.

problems.name: Maximum Average Subarray I
problems.note: The canonical fixed-size window: add the entering element, drop the leaving one.

problems.name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.note: Variable window with a last-seen map — the OA staple of this whole module.

problems.name: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
problems.note: Shortest-valid window: shrink **while** `sum >= target` and record inside the shrink loop.

problems.name: Container With Most Water
problems.note: Opposite pointers — always move the shorter wall, and know the greedy proof (deep dive 2).

problems.name: 3Sum
problems.note: Sort + fix one element + opposite pointers for the pair — and dedupe on *both* pointers.

problems.name: Permutation in String
problems.note: Fixed window of size `|s1|` plus a 26-slot frequency match (anagram check).

problems.name: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
problems.note: Window valid while `(len - maxFreq) <= k`; the stale-`maxFreq` shortcut is safe (deep dive 3).

problems.name: Fruit Into Baskets
problems.note: Longest window with at most 2 distinct — the at-most-K template with K hardcoded to 2.

problems.name: Subarrays with K Different Integers
problems.note: The at-most-K subtraction trick: `exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1)` (deep dive 1).

problems.name: Minimum Window Substring
problems.note: The boss problem: shortest window covering a required multiset — track `have` vs `need` counts.

deepdives.q: **Subarrays with K Different Integers** — given an integer array, count the subarrays that contain **exactly** K distinct integers.
**Container With Most Water** — choose two vertical lines from a height array; they hold water of area `min(height[lo], height[hi]) * (hi - lo)`. Maximize that area.
**Longest Repeating Character Replacement** — given an uppercase string and an integer k, find the longest substring you can make all-one-character by replacing **at most k** characters.
**Minimum Size Subarray Sum** — given a target and an array of positive integers, find the length of the shortest contiguous subarray whose sum is at least the target (0 if none exists).

quiz.q: An OA asks: *given a string, find the length of the longest substring containing at most K distinct characters.* Which approach fits best, and why?
options: Sort the characters, then use opposite pointers from both ends
DP over prefixes: `dp[i]` = longest valid substring ending at `i`
Variable-size sliding window with a frequency map, shrinking while the distinct count exceeds K
Binary search on the answer length, checking each candidate length with a linear scan
quiz.explanation: Substring means contiguous, and *at most K distinct* is a monotone invariant (shrinking a valid window keeps it valid) — exactly the shrink-when-invalid window, $O(n)$. Sorting destroys contiguity, so option A answers a different problem. Binary search + check (option D) actually works but costs $O(n \log n)$ and is strictly dominated. DP adds machinery the window makes unnecessary.

quiz.q: The variable-window template has a `for` loop over `right` containing a `while` loop over `left`. Why is the total runtime $O(n)$ rather than $O(n^2)$?
options: Each index enters the window once and leaves at most once, so total pointer movement is bounded by 2n
The while loop runs at most once per for iteration
The window size is bounded by the constant K
The inner loop only runs when the window is valid, which is rare
quiz.explanation: This is an amortized argument: `left` never moves backwards, so across the *entire* run it advances at most n times — even if one particular iteration shrinks many steps. Option B is false for the general template (that one-step-per-iteration behavior is the special `if`-based variant in Longest Repeating Character Replacement); options C and D describe properties the template does not rely on.

quiz.q: Your window currently spans `left = 2`, `right = 5`, and that window is the true optimum. A teammate's code records `best = max(best, right - left)`. What does it return for this window?
options: 4 — the code is correct
3 — one less than the correct length of 4
5 — one more than the correct length
It depends on the array contents
quiz.explanation: A window from index 2 to index 5 inclusive contains indices 2, 3, 4, 5 — four elements, so the length is `right - left + 1 = 4`. Dropping the `+ 1` yields 3. This off-by-one never crashes; it just quietly loses points, which is why you memorize `right - left + 1` as a single unit.

quiz.q: You need to **count** the subarrays containing **exactly** K odd numbers. The cleanest $O(n)$ approach is:
options: A single sliding window that expands and shrinks to maintain exactly K odd numbers
A stack of the positions of odd numbers, popped as the window slides
Fix each left endpoint and scan right until the K-th odd number appears
Compute `atMost(K) - atMost(K-1)`, where `atMost` slides a window and adds `right - left + 1` at each step
quiz.explanation: *Exactly K* is not a monotone window invariant, so a single window (option A) has no clean shrink condition. The subtraction identity converts it into two monotone at-most windows, each counting all valid subarrays ending at `right` via `right - left + 1`. Option C is a correct but $O(n^2)$-worst-case brute force; option B does not lead anywhere coherent.

quiz.q: Trace the fixed-size window template on `a = [3, -1, 4, 2]` with `k = 2`. What maximum window sum does it return?
quiz.explanation: The complete size-2 windows are `[3,-1] = 2`, `[-1,4] = 3`, and `[4,2] = 6`, so the answer is 6. The tempting 7 comes from pairing 3 and 4 — but they are not adjacent, and a window is contiguous by definition. If you picked 4, you likely recorded before the window was full (the `i >= k - 1` guard).

quiz.q: In Container With Most Water, the pointers sit at walls of height `h[lo] = 4` and `h[hi] = 9`. Why does the algorithm move `lo` rather than `hi`?
options: Because `lo` has been stationary longer, and alternating the pointers guarantees O(n)
Because moving `hi` shrinks the width while the area stays capped by the shorter wall `h[lo]` — it can never produce a better area
Because taller walls must always be kept until the end of the scan
Because moving `lo` is guaranteed to increase `min(h[lo], h[hi])`
quiz.explanation: This is the exchange argument: with the shorter wall fixed at height 4, every pair reachable by moving `hi` inward has smaller width and height still capped at 4 — provably no better. Moving `lo` merely *might* help, which is enough. Option D is wrong because the next wall could be even shorter; the algorithm does not need a guaranteed improvement, only that the discarded options are provably hopeless.

quiz.q: The shrink-while-valid solution to Minimum Size Subarray Sum is correct for positive arrays. Which single change to the input breaks it?
options: Allowing negative numbers — removing an element can then increase the sum, so validity is no longer monotone and the sweep can miss answers
A target larger than the total array sum — the while loop never fires and the code crashes
Duplicate values — the frequency map overcounts distinct elements
A single-element array — `right - left + 1` underflows
quiz.explanation: The window logic relies on adding elements only growing the sum and removing them only shrinking it; negatives destroy both directions, and the fix is prefix sums with a monotonic deque. Option B is handled gracefully (best stays `INT_MAX`, the function returns 0 — no crash). Option C is irrelevant since this problem uses a running sum, not a frequency map, and option D describes an expression that is fine at size 1.

quiz.q: In Longest Repeating Character Replacement, the shrink step is a plain `if`, and `maxFreq` is never recomputed downward after `left++`. Why is the final answer still correct?
options: The `if` can run at most k times over the whole string
`maxFreq` is implicitly recomputed by `++cnt[s[right] - 'A']` on the next iteration
It is not strictly correct — it is an approximation that LeetCode happens to accept
A stale `maxFreq` only makes the validity check lenient; it can never certify a new longer window, because `best` was already recorded when `maxFreq` was genuinely that high
quiz.explanation: The answer is monotone: you only care about windows *longer* than `best`, and a longer valid window requires a genuinely higher `maxFreq` — which is exactly when the code updates it. A stale value can leave the window one step too large, but never mints a longer wrong answer. Option B is false (`max` only ever raises it for the entering character), and the algorithm is exactly correct, not approximate.

m02-opposite-pointers.caption: Opposite pointers on the sorted array `[1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 11]` with target 10. The current sum `1 + 11 = 12` overshoots, so `hi` steps left — each comparison permanently discards one index, giving $O(n)$.

m02-window-shrink.caption: The shrink-when-invalid loop on `[1, 2, 1, 3, 2]` with the invariant *at most 2 distinct*. Pulling in `a[3] = 3` makes 3 distinct values (top, invalid); `left` advances to 2 and the window `{1, 3}` is valid again (bottom). Record `best` only after the shrink loop.

m02-fixed-window.caption: A fixed window of size `k = 3` sliding one step: `a[4]` enters on the right while `a[1]` leaves on the left. The `i >= k` guard controls the subtraction; the `i >= k - 1` guard controls recording.

m02-count-ending-right.caption: The counting idiom inside `atMost`: with a valid window from `left = 1` to `right = 4`, every start index from 1 through 4 gives a valid subarray ending at `right` — four of them — so the code adds `right - left + 1 = 4`.

x2="244" y2="236" class="s-accent" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="70" y1="258" x2="244" y2="258" class="s-accent" stroke-width="3"/><text x="290" y="196" class="t-sm">subarrays ending at right:</text><text x="290" y="224" class="t-mono t-sm">[4..4] [3..4] [2..4] [1..4]</text><text x="290" y="254" class="t-mono">res += right - left + 1 = 4</text></svg>

m02-shorter-wall.caption: Container With Most Water: the trapped area (shaded) is capped by the **shorter** wall at `lo`. Moving the taller `hi` wall inward only shrinks the width while the cap stays — so the only move worth making is discarding `lo`.
m02-shorter-wall.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 280" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Water held between two walls, capped by the shorter one"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">move the shorter wall</text><rect x="86" y="150" width="374" height="60" class="f-accent-soft o-60"/><rect x="60" y="150" width="26" height="60" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><rect x="160" y="70" width="26" height="140" class="f-panel s-line"/><rect x="260" y="170" width="26" height="40" class="f-panel s-line"/><rect x="360" y="110" width="26" height="100" class="f-panel s-line"/><rect x="460" y="90" width="26" height="120" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><line x1="40" y1="210" x2="620" y2="210" class="s-line" stroke-width="2"/><text x="73" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">lo (h=3)</text><text x="473" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">hi (h=6)</text><text x="20" y="262" class="t-sm">area = min(3, 6) x width — only discarding lo can possibly do better</text></svg>
